old_claim.note: Codified Law provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Bodin's sovereignty formulation grew from legal-humanist and civil-law scholarship, making formal legal-system traditions contextual infrastructure rather than an ancient law-code prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Life and Methodus sections: IEP says Bodin studied and taught law at Toulouse, worked as an advocate at the Parlement of Paris, and combined historical and legal study with legal-humanist methods.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: IEP roots Bodin's political theory in legal scholarship and legal-humanist study rather than ancient Mesopotamian law codes.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The existing legal_systems_common_civil node captures the formal civil-law/common-law legal-system infrastructure better than the ancient codified_law node.

ontology_before: Bodin's sovereignty theory depended directly on ancient written law codes, laundering Ur-Nammu evidence into an early modern French legal-political theory.
ontology_after: Bodin's sovereignty theory depends on formal legal-system traditions as contextual legal infrastructure for a jurist's sovereignty doctrine.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: codified_law was replaced by legal_systems_common_civil for sovereignty_theory_bodin.
would_reject_if: The Bodin node were narrowed to generic ancient law-code influence rather than early modern sovereignty doctrine.
A stronger source showed ancient Mesopotamian law codes were a direct prerequisite to Bodin's 1576 theory.
The legal_systems_common_civil node were narrowed away from Roman civil-law traditions and formal legal procedure.
short_reason: Bodin's source context is civil-law/legal-humanist scholarship, not ancient law-code evidence.
